content.full_text:
CALVIN P. SCHMIDT
JUDGE

FPPC

JAN 29 8 09 AM '90

January 19, 1990

Mr. John Rothman
Fair Political Practices Commission
Legal Division
428 J Street, Suite 800
Sacramento,CA  95814

Dear Mr. Rothman:

Thank you so much for the time you have taken to assist
me in solving my dilemma.

The basic facts are:

That I am seeking to raise funds in the amount of $80,000
plus dollars. I will use these funds to pay the legal expenses
that I incurred while defending myself against the charges leveled
by the Judicial Performance Commission. The charges directly
related to my job. It is not a criminal charge, or civil, but
rather an administrative one in nature. The Judicial Canon
of Ethics, as I read them, prohibit me from holding any type of
fundraiser unless it be during a campaign for re-election.
There is a provision that provides for gifts to a Judge at any time,
as long as certain requirements are met. Furthermore, attorneys
may not contribute.

Some friends of mine wish to hold a belated birthday party
for me, and any funds received would not be earmarked or restricted
in any way, and the donor would be made aware that this would be
a gift; thus, I would be free to use the money in a manner of
my choosing. Attorneys are prohibited from making such gifts.

As I understand the regulations of the Fair Political
Practices Commission, that any such fundraiser or party would be
considered a political campaign contribution, and thus I would
have to comply with the appropriate regulations, i.e. Committee
numbers, Treasurer, etc.

The problem lies in that the Judicial Performance Commission
says that I cannot do this, and on the other hand, the Fair Political
Practices Commission says that it is a campaign type fundraiser not
a gift. How do I handle the situation?

In either event, I am more than happy to disclose, on
any form that is necessary, the donor, the amount, address, etc.

Mr. John Rothman                    - 2 -                    January 19, 1990


     I have requested an advisory letter from the Judicial Performance
Ethics Committee as to the appropriateness of raising funds through
the gift method.  They have some concern about this, but I explained
to them that if I was to be so prohibited, that I would effectively
be denied access to monies to defend myself.  I might note that the
cost of the litigation exceeded my year's gross salary.

     As I said, monies are still owed.  My address is:

               P. O. Box 9686
               Newport Beach, CA  92660

     I am a judge of the Harbor Municipal Court, 4601 Jamboree Road,
Newport Beach, CA  92660, and my office telephone number is
(714) 476-4688.  However, I prefer my mailing address to be the
P. O. Box 9686, Newport Beach address above.

     I look forward to your response.

                                        Very truly yours,

                                        [signature]
                                        CALVIN P. SCHMIDT

CPS:gem

sections.question: The problem lies in that the Judicial Performance Commission says that I cannot do this, and on the other hand, the Fair political Practics Commission says that it is a campaign type fundraiser not a gift. How do I handle the situation?
sections.question_synthetic: A judge seeks to raise $80,000 through a gift-based fundraiser (disguised as a birthday party) to pay legal expenses from defending against Judicial Performance Commission charges, but faces conflicting guidance: Judicial Canon of Ethics prohibits fundraisers outside campaigns while allowing unrestricted gifts, whereas FPPC regulations would treat this as a political campaign contribution requiring disclosure and committee registration.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: This is an incoming request letter from a judge to FPPC staff (John Rothman), not an FPPC response. No formal QUESTION or CONCLUSION sections present. Judge seeks advisory letter on permissibility of fundraising under both FPPC and Judicial Canon rules. Document shows date discrepancy in header (August 23 vs January 19, 1990).

embedding.qa_text: QUESTION: The problem lies in that the Judicial Performance Commission says that I cannot do this, and on the other hand, the Fair political Practics Commission says that it is a campaign type fundraiser not a gift. How do I handle the situation?

embedding.summary: Judge Calvin P. Schmidt requests guidance on whether he can raise funds through a gift-based birthday party to cover legal defense costs, caught between conflicting regulatory interpretations from the Judicial Performance Commission and FPPC.
